p_question-10743.ja:
以下は留学生せいの作文である。


カラオケ

マイヤー　レア

　私は歌を歌うのが好きで、友達やゼミの仲間とときどきカラオケに行きます。いつも大おお勢せいで行くから、自分一人がずっと歌うということはできません。[19]、カラオケはパーティーみたいで楽しいと思っていました。

　先月、桜駅の近くで「一人カラオケ専門店」という店を見つけました。私はとても驚きました。カラオケは人と一緒に楽しむものだと[20]。次の日、学校で友達にその店のことを話すと、友達は普通のカラオケ店にも一人で行くと言ったので、もっと驚きました。カラオケに一人で行くというのは、考えたこともありませんでした。

　一人で行ってもいいとわかって、私も一人で[21]。

　私はどきどきしながら、受付で名前を書きました。そして、[22]に入り、まだ上手に歌えない日本語の歌を練習したり、好きな歌を繰返し歌ったりしました。いろいろな歌を自由にゆうに歌うのは気持ちがよかったです。一人で２時間も歌ってしまいました。

　カラオケは一人[23]楽しかったです。これからもっとカラオケに行くことが増えそうです。

This is an essay written by an international student.

Karaoke

Mayer Lea

I like singing, and I sometimes go to karaoke with my friends or seminar group members. Since we always go in a large group, I can't sing the whole time by myself. [19] I thought karaoke was fun, like a party.

Last month, I found a shop near Sakura Station called a “solo karaoke specialty store.” I was very surprised. I had believed that karaoke was something you enjoy together with other people [20]. The next day, when I told my friends at school about the shop, I was even more surprised because they said they also go alone to regular karaoke places. I had never even thought about going to karaoke alone.

Realizing that it was okay to go alone, I also went by myself [21].

My heart was pounding as I wrote my name at the reception. Then I entered [22] and practiced Japanese songs I still can’t sing well, and repeatedly sang the songs I like. It felt great to freely sing many different songs. I ended up singing alone for two whole hours.

Karaoke was fun even [23] by myself. It seems like I will be going to karaoke more often from now on.

p_question-10748.ja:
（１）

　姉に女の子が生まれた。子どもの名前は「はるか」だそうだ。日本人の名前は漢字を使ったものが多く、ひらがなだけの名前は珍しい。小さいころから自分で書けるからそうしたのかと思って、姉に聞いてみた。すると、ひらがななら、優しい感じがするからという答えだった。読み間違えられることもないし、覚えてもらいやすいという良い点もあるだろう。ひらがなの名前もいいなと思った。

(1)

My older sister had a baby girl. She said the child's name is “Haruka.” Many Japanese names use kanji, so names written only in hiragana are unusual. I thought she chose it because her child could write it easily from a young age, so I asked her. She answered that hiragana gives a gentle impression. There are also good points such as not being misread and being easy to remember. I thought hiragana names are nice too.

p_question-10749.ja:
（２）

これは、まり子さんが友達のラナさんに書いた手紙である。


ラナさん

こんにちは。

　コンサートのチケットを送ります。来週の金曜日、楽しみですね。

　実は、当日、急な仕事が入ってしまいました。この前のコンサートのときは駅の改札口で待ち合わせましたが、今回は直接会場に行って、先に中に入っていてくれませんか。がんばって仕事を終わらせるようにしますが、遅れそうだったら、メールしますね。

　コンサートは18時半開始ですが、会場には18時から入れます。

　では、金曜日に。

まり子

(2)

This is a letter Mariko wrote to her friend Lana.

Lana,

Hello.

I’m sending you the concert tickets. I’m looking forward to next Friday.

Actually, a sudden job came up for me on that day. Last time we met at the ticket gate at the station, but this time could you go directly to the venue and go inside first? I’ll try my best to finish work, but if I look like I’ll be late, I’ll email you.

The concert starts at 6:30 p.m., but you can enter the venue from 6:00 p.m.

See you on Friday.

Mariko

p_question-10750.ja:
（３）

これは文房具店が客の寺市さんに送ったメールである。


寺市商店寺市様

　いつもお世話になっております。

　ご注文の商品を昨日お送りしたのですが、間違いがございました。大変申し訳ありません。ご注文をいただいていたのは、コピー用紙１箱、のり５本でしたが、のりは３本しかお送りしておりませんでした。

　今朝、急いで残りをお送りいたしましたので、明日の朝には届く予定です。

　ご迷惑をおかけして申し訳ありません。

ナカノ文房具中野

(3)

This is an email sent from a stationery shop to their customer, Mr. Teraichi.

Teraichi Store
Mr. Teraichi

Thank you for your continued business.

We sent your ordered items yesterday, but there was a mistake. We sincerely apologize. You ordered one box of copy paper and five glue sticks, but we only sent three glue sticks.

We sent the remaining items urgently this morning, so they are expected to arrive tomorrow morning.

We are very sorry for the inconvenience.

Nakano Stationery
Nakano

p_question-10751.ja:
（４）

　私は鉄道の旅が好きだ。特に、田舎をゆっくり走る鉄道の旅は、外の景色を楽しめて最高だ。日本ではかなりの田舎でも鉄道が通っている。人が少ない田舎にまで作られたのは、自動車より前に鉄道が移動手段として広く使われるようになったからだそうだ。もし順番が逆だったら、私は今のように田舎の鉄道の旅を楽しめなかっただろう。この歴史に感謝したい。

(4)

I like traveling by train. In particular, traveling on trains that move slowly through the countryside is wonderful because you can enjoy the scenery outside. In Japan, trains run even in very rural areas. I heard that the reason railways were built even in places with few people is that trains became widely used as a means of transportation before cars. If the order had been reversed, I probably wouldn’t be able to enjoy rural train trips as I do now. I am grateful for this history.

p_question-10752.ja:
（１）

「よろしくお願いします。これ、私の名刺です。」

　先日、あるパーティーで、袋に入った１枚のクッキーを渡された。①「えっ」と思って、よく見ると、クッキーの表に名前と会社名が書いてあった。相手の人は笑いながら「食べられますよ。」と言った。

　名刺は普通、四角い紙でできている。しかし、最近は変わった名刺を持つ人もいる。会社が作っている商品と同じ形をしたもの、木やプラスチックでできたもの、香りがついたものもあるそうだ。

　名刺は仕事には必要なものだが、②ほかの人と同じような名刺では、顔や名前をなかなか覚えてもらえない。クッキーの名刺をくれた人によると、このような名刺にすると、すぐに名前を覚えてもらえるだけでなく、話が広がり、仕事の話がうまくいく場合もあるそうだ。

　まず、名刺から相手の心をつかむ。これが、最近の新しい仕事のやり方なのかもしれない。

(1)

“Nice to meet you. Here is my business card.”

Recently, at a party, someone handed me a cookie wrapped in a small bag. ①I thought, “What?” and looked more closely. On the surface of the cookie, the person’s name and company were written. Smiling, the person said, “You can eat it.”

Business cards are usually made of rectangular paper. However, recently some people carry unusual business cards. Some are shaped like the products their company makes, some are made of wood or plastic, and some are even scented.

Business cards are necessary for work, but ②if your business card is the same as everyone else’s, people won’t easily remember your face or name. According to the person who gave me the cookie business card, unique business cards not only help people remember your name quickly, but also start conversations and sometimes even help business go smoothly.

First, capture the other person’s interest with your business card. This may be the new way of working these days.

p_question-10755.ja:
（２）

　子供はほめて育てろとよく聞きます。でも私が息子をほめると、息子はその時の自分に満足して、それ以上頑張ろうとしなくなることもあります。親の期待の通りにはならないことが多く、ほめ方の難しさを感じています。

　最近、どうほめたら子供が新しいことにチャレンジする気持ちを持つようになるか、調べた実験の話を聞きました。数百人の子供に難しい問題を１０問解かせます。そして、良い結果だった子供たちを二つのグループに分け、一方に「よくできたね。頭がいいね。」と「頭の良さ」を、もう一方に「よく頑張ったね。」と「努力」をほめる言葉をかけます。すると、次の行動に①違いが現れました。

　実験では続けて、同じ問題をもう一度解くか、新しい問題をやってみるかを選ばせます。そうしたら、頭の良さをほめられたグループでは、間違えると頭が悪いと思われてしまうと考えたからか、多くの子供が同じ問題を解きました。それに対して、努力をほめられたグループでは、９０％が新しい問題にチャレンジしたのです。

　②ほめ方のヒントが隠された面白い実験だと思いました。

(2)

We often hear that children should be raised with praise. But when I praise my son, he sometimes becomes satisfied with himself and doesn’t try to work harder. Things don’t always go the way parents expect, and I find praising difficult.

Recently I heard about an experiment that studied how to praise children so they will want to challenge new things. Hundreds of children solved ten difficult problems. The children who did well were divided into two groups. One group was told, “Good job. You’re smart,” praising their “intelligence,” and the other group was told, “Good job. You worked hard,” praising their “effort.” Then, ①a difference appeared in their next actions.

They were then asked to choose whether to solve the same problems again or try new ones. Many children in the group praised for intelligence solved the same problems again, perhaps because they feared being seen as “not smart” if they got something wrong. In contrast, 90% of the children praised for effort tried the new problems.

②I thought it was an interesting experiment that revealed hints about how to praise effectively.

p_question-10758.ja:
最近、日本では夏の平均気温が上がってきていて、非常に暑い日はエアコンがなければ何もできない。そんな日は、ほとんどの人がエアコンのスイッチを入れる。ひどい暑さは体にも悪い影響を与えるし、①しかたないことだ。しかし、それぞれの家に何台もエアコンがある今、皆が同時にエアコンを使うと、電気の使用量が増えて電気が足りなくなる心配がある。

　そこで、できるだけ夏の電気の消費量を抑えようという工夫や活動が日本の各地で始まっている。

　夏に気温が高くなることで有名なK市では、毎年６月から９月まで、「②家のエアコンを止めて、涼しい所に行こう。」と呼びかけている。例えば、木々の多い公園や神社、寺、エアコンのある図書館やレストランやデパートなど、涼しい場所に集まって時間を過ごすのだ。そうすれば、町の中で同時に使われるエアコンはずっと減る。市では、市内の涼しい場所や、この活動に協力してくれる店などが載っている③地図も作り、すべての家に配っている。この地図にある店へ行って、家のエアコンを止めて来たと言えば、飲み物の無料サービスが受けられたり、商品の割引サービスが受けられたりする。

　電気消費量を抑える効果を期待して始まった活動だが、町の人々がお互いに交流する機会が増え、前よりもっとコミュニケーションが盛んになるという効果も出ているそうだ。

Recently, in Japan the average summer temperature has been rising, and on extremely hot days people can’t do anything without air conditioning. On such days, most people turn on their air conditioners. Severe heat has harmful effects on the body, so ①it can’t be helped. However, now that many homes have multiple air conditioners, if everyone uses them at the same time, electricity consumption increases and there is a concern that there won’t be enough electricity.

Therefore, various efforts and activities to reduce electricity consumption in summer have begun in many parts of Japan.

In City K, which is known for its high summer temperatures, from June to September every year they encourage people to “②Turn off your home air conditioner and go somewhere cool.” For example, people gather and spend time in cool places such as tree-filled parks, shrines, temples, libraries, restaurants, and department stores with air conditioning. Doing so greatly reduces the number of air conditioners being used at the same time in the city. The city also makes a ③map listing cool places and shops that cooperate with the activity, and distributes it to all households. If you go to a shop listed on the map and say you turned off your home air conditioner, you may receive a free drink or a discount on products.

The activity was started with the expectation of reducing electricity usage, but it is also said to have had the effect of increasing interaction among townspeople and making communication livelier than before.

questions.stem: 中村部長と直接話した。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 3: ちょくせつ is correct.

Question Translation
「中村部長と直接話した。」→ I spoke directly with Manager Nakamura.

Explanation
The kanji 「直接」 is read ちょくせつ and means directly. Here it shows that the speaker talked to 中村部長 without going through someone else, so ちょくせつ is the only correct reading.

Other Options
1. ちょくぜつ – Not a standard reading of 「直接」; this word does not exist in normal Japanese.
2. ちょうせつ – Reading of 「調節」 (adjustment), different kanji and meaning.
4. ちょうぜつ – Reading of 「超絶」 (extraordinary, extreme), also different kanji and meaning.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
「直接」 (ちょくせつ) is often used with verbs like 話す, 会う, 見る to mean directly, without an intermediary.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「直接(ちょくせつ)」 — 직접. 사람을 거치지 않고 부장과 직접 이야기했다는 의미.
오답: 1 ちょくぜつ·4 ちょうぜつ는 없는 음. 2 ちょうせつ는 「調節」(조절)과 헷갈리기 쉬운 함정.
포인트: 直=チョク, 接=セツ.

questions.stem: これはすぐには燃えません。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 4: もえません is correct.

Question Translation
「これはすぐには燃えません。」→ This will not burn easily.

Explanation
The kanji 「燃える」 means to burn. In the sentence, 燃えません is the polite negative form, will not burn. It describes a material that does not catch fire quickly, so もえません is the correct reading.

Other Options
1. にえません – Reading of 「煮えません」 (will not boil), different verb and meaning.
2. ひえません – Reading of 「冷えません」 (does not get cold), different kanji.
3. きえません – Reading of 「消えません」 (does not go out, does not disappear), also different kanji and meaning.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
Verb ます-stem + ません is the polite present negative. 燃える → 燃えません (does not burn).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「燃(も)えません」 — 타지 않다. 불이 잘 붙지 않는 재료를 묘사.
오답: 1 にえません는 「煮える」(끓다), 2 ひえません는 「冷える」(차가워지다), 3 きえません는 「消える」(꺼지다).
포인트: 燃える=もえる(자동사).

questions.stem: この位置に車を止めてください。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 4: いち is correct.

Question Translation
「この位置に車を止めてください。」→ Please park the car in this position.

Explanation
「位置」 means position, location and is read いち. The sentence talks about the position where you should park the car, so いち is the correct reading for this kanji.

Other Options
1. とじ – Reading of 「閉じ」 (to close), unrelated kanji and meaning.
2. いじ – Can be the reading of 「維持」 (maintenance), not 「位置」.
3. とち – Reading of 「土地」 (land), different kanji.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
「位置」 (いち) is used for a specific point or spot. A similar word is 場所 (ばしょ), but 場所 is more general, while 位置 is often more precise or technical.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「位置(いち)」 — 위치. 차를 세울 자리.
오답: 1 とじ는 「閉じ」, 2 いじ는 「維持」(유지), 3 とち는 「土地」(토지)와 혼동을 유도.
포인트: 位=イ, 置=チ.

questions.stem: 計算ができたので見てください。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 3: けいさん is correct.

Question Translation
「計算ができたので見てください。」→ I have finished the calculation, so please look.

Explanation
「計算」 means calculation, computation and is normally read けいさん in modern Japanese. Here it refers to a finished calculation, so けいさん is the natural reading.

Other Options
1. けいかく – Reading of 「計画」 (plan), different word.
2. けいざん – An old or specialized reading of 計算 in some contexts (abacus use), but not the common N3-level reading here.
4. けいがく – Not a standard reading of 「計算」; as a word it is uncommon and does not match this kanji.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
「計算する」 means to calculate, do a calculation. Common phrases: 電卓で計算する, 頭の中で計算する.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「計算(けいさん)」 — 계산. 끝낸 계산을 봐달라는 의미.
오답: 1 けいかく는 「計画」(계획)이라 혼동 주의. 2 けいざん·4 けいがく는 없는 음.
포인트: 算=サン(탁음 없음).

questions.stem: ずっとパソコンを使っていて疲れたので、腕を回してみた。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 1: まわして is correct.

Question Translation
「ずっとパソコンを使っていて疲れたので、腕を回してみた。」→ I was tired from using the computer for a long time, so I tried rotating my arms.

Explanation
「回す」 (まわす) means to turn, rotate. In て-form it is 回して (まわして). Here the person is rotating their arms to relax them, so まわして is the correct reading.

Other Options
2. おして – Reading of 「押して」 (to push), wrong verb.
3. おろして – Reading of 「下ろして」 (to lower, to take down), different meaning.
4. のばして – Reading of 「伸ばして」 (to stretch), also possible in a different sentence but does not match the kanji 回して.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
Verb て-form + みる (回してみた) expresses trying something to see what happens: tried rotating (my arms).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「回(まわ)して」 — 돌리다. 피곤해서 팔을 돌려본다.
오답: 2 おして는 「押して」(누르다), 3 おろして는 「下ろして」(내리다), 4 のばして는 「伸ばして」(펴다).
포인트: 回す=まわす(타동사), 回る=まわる(자동사).

questions.stem: ここは禁煙です。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 4: きんえん is correct.

Question Translation
「ここは禁煙です。」→ Smoking is prohibited here.

Explanation
「禁煙」 means no smoking, smoking prohibited and is read きんえん. It is used on signs and notices where smoking is not allowed, so きんえん is the correct reading.

Other Options
1. きねん – Usually 「記念」 (commemoration) or 「忌念」, not 禁煙.
2. きえん – Not a common word for 禁煙; this reading is not used here.
3. きんねん – Usually 「近年」 (recent years), different kanji.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
Place + は禁煙です is a very common pattern for saying smoking is not allowed in that area.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「禁煙(きんえん)」 — 금연. 표지판·안내 표현.
오답: 1 きねん는 「記念」(기념), 2 きえん·3 きんねん은 없는 음.
포인트: 禁=キン(ん 발음), 煙=エン.

questions.stem: きれいに結んでください。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 2: むすんで is correct.

Question Translation
「きれいに結んでください。」→ Please tie it neatly.

Explanation
「結ぶ」 (むすぶ) means to tie, fasten, knot. In て-form it becomes 結んで (むすんで). The sentence asks someone to tie something nicely, so むすんで is correct.

Other Options
1. たたんで – Reading of 「畳んで」 (fold), not tie.
3. つつんで – Reading of 「包んで」 (wrap), wrong verb.
4. ならんで – Reading of 「並んで」 (line up), different meaning.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
結ぶ is often used for tying things like shoelaces, hair, or a ribbon: 靴ひもを結ぶ, 髪を結ぶ.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「結(むす)んで」 — 묶다. 「きれいに結ぶ」(예쁘게 묶다).
오답: 1 たたんで는 「畳んで」(개다), 3 つつんで는 「包んで」(싸다), 4 ならんで는 「並んで」(줄서다).
포인트: 結ぶ=むすぶ.

questions.stem: 手術をしなくても治りますから、大丈夫ですよ。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 1: しゅじゅつ is correct.

Question Translation
「手術をしなくても治りますから、大丈夫ですよ。」→ You will get better even without surgery, so it is all right.

Explanation
「手術」 means surgery, operation and is read しゅじゅつ. The doctor is saying that the illness will heal without needing surgery, so しゅじゅつ is the correct reading.

Other Options
2. てじゅつ – Not a standard reading of 手術.
3. しゅしゅつ – Also not a natural reading; this form is not used.
4. てしゅつ – Not a valid reading.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
The pattern Verb なくても大丈夫 expresses that something is fine even if you do not do that action, e.g. 手術をしなくても大丈夫.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「手術(しゅじゅつ)」 — 수술. 수술하지 않아도 낫는다는 의사의 말.
오답: 2 てじゅつ·3 しゅしゅつ·4 てしゅつ는 없는 음. 手를 「て」로 잘못 읽기 쉬운 함정.
포인트: 手=シュ(음독), 術=ジュツ.

questions.stem: 次の目標は10びょうです。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 4 is correct. It uses 秒, which means second (unit of time).

Question Translation
「次の目標は10びょうです。」→ My next goal is 10 seconds.

Explanation
Here びょう is the unit of time seconds, so the correct kanji is 秒. Therefore option 4, 秒, is the correct choice.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 培 – Means cultivate, foster (for example, 経験を培う). It cannot be used as the time unit びょう.
2. 抄 – Means "excerpt" or "extract."
3. 倍 – Means times, multiple (for example, 2倍 = two times). It does not mean second as a time unit.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
秒 (びょう) is the standard kanji for seconds of time: 一秒, 十秒. Be careful not to confuse it with 分 (minutes) or 時 (hours).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「秒(びょう)」 — 초(시간 단위). 「10びょう」=10초.
오답: 1 培(기르다), 2 抄(베끼다), 3 倍(배)는 의미가 맞지 않음.
포인트: 시간 단위 秒=びょう.

questions.stem: ボールが遠くまでとんだ。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 3 is correct. It means 飛んだ (flew).

Question Translation
「ボールが遠くまでとんだ。」→ The ball flew far.

Explanation
Here とんだ describes the ball moving through the air to a distant place, so the kanji 飛んだ is appropriate.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 投んだ – 投ぶ is not a normal verb form; 投げた (threw) would be correct Japanese. Also, the meaning is throw, not fly.
2. 打んだ – 打ぶ is not a standard verb; 打った (hit) is the normal past form. It means hit, not flew.
4. 転んだ – Means fell over, tripped (from 転ぶ). It describes a person or object falling, not flying through the air.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
飛ぶ → 飛んだ is the past form of to fly or jump. It is commonly used with things like ボールが飛ぶ or 鳥が飛ぶ.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「飛(と)んだ」 — 날다. 공이 멀리까지 날아간 상황.
오답: 1 投(던지다)는 「投げた」가 자연스럽고 訓도 안 맞음. 2 打(치다)도 활용형 불일치. 4 転んだ는 「ころんだ」(넘어지다)로 읽음.
포인트: 飛ぶ=とぶ.

questions.stem: ここは、さかみちが多いですね。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 4 is correct. It means 坂道 (slope, hill road).

Question Translation
「ここは、さかみちが多いですね。」→ There are many slopes (hilly roads) around here, aren’t there.

Explanation
さかみち refers to a road that goes up or down, so 坂道 (slope road) is the correct kanji.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 岩道 – Means rocky road or path with rocks (岩 = rock). It does not specifically mean slope.
2. 板道 – Would literally mean board road (板 = board), which is not a natural word for さかみち.
3. 石道 – Would mean stone road (石 = stone). Again, this does not express the idea of a slope.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
坂道 (さかみち) is a common word for streets that go uphill or downhill. 坂 (さか) alone also means slope: 坂を上る, 坂を下りる.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「坂道(さかみち)」 — 비탈길. 오르내리는 길.
오답: 1 岩道(바위길), 2 板道(없는 말), 3 石道(돌길)는 さかみち가 아님.
포인트: 坂=さか, 道=みち.

questions.stem: この皿だけ色がちがいます。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 4 is correct. It means 違います (is different).

Question Translation
「この皿だけ色がちがいます。」→ Only this plate is a different color.

Explanation
The verb ちがいます (to be different) is written 違います, so option 4 is the correct spelling here.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
Answer Choice 1 (遺います): Uses the kanji for "bequeath" or "leave behind" (as in a will).
Answer Choice 2 (達います): Uses the kanji for "reach" or "attain."
Answer Choice 3 (遅います): Uses the kanji for "late" or "slow."

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
X は Y と違います means X is different from Y. It is a very common expression to compare things: 味が違います, 考え方が違います.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「違(ちが)います」 — 다르다. 색이 다른 접시.
오답: 1 遺(남기다), 2 達(이르다), 3 遅(늦다)는 모양이 비슷한 한자 함정.
포인트: 違う=ちがう.

questions.stem: あの会社とは、いいかんけいをつくりたい。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 2 is correct. It means 関係 (relationship, connection).

Question Translation
「あの会社とは、いいかんけいをつくりたい。」→ I want to build a good relationship with that company.

Explanation
かんけい in the sense of human or business relationships is written 関係, so option 2 is correct.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 関系 – 系 is used in words like 系統 (system, lineage). 関系 is not a correct kanji combination for かんけい.
3. 間係 – The first kanji 間 (between) is wrong here; the correct one is 関.
4. 間系 – This combination does not represent the word かんけい in Japanese.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
関係をつくる means to build a relationship, especially a business or human connection. Similar expressions: いい関係を保つ (maintain a good relationship).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「関係(かんけい)」 — 관계. 좋은 관계를 만들고 싶다.
오답: 1 関系·3 間係·4 間系는 잘못된 한자 조합.
포인트: 係=ケイ(イ에 주의), 関係는 빈출어.

questions.stem: 父はきょうしをしています。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 3 is correct. It means 教師 (teacher).

Question Translation
「父はきょうしをしています。」→ My father works as a teacher.

Explanation
きょうし meaning teacher (as a profession) is written 教師, so option 3 is correct.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 教仕 – 仕 is used in 仕事 (work), but 教仕 is not a natural word.
2. 教帥 – 帥 appears in words like 統帥 (command), not in きょうし meaning teacher.
4. 教任 – 任 is used in 赴任, 就任 etc. 教任 is not the standard word for teacher.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
教師 is a formal word for teacher as an occupation. 先生 is more general and is also used as a title when addressing teachers, doctors, etc.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「教師(きょうし)」 — 교사. 아버지의 직업.
오답: 1 教仕·2 教帥·4 教任은 「師」와 모양이 비슷해 헷갈리기 쉬운 함정.
포인트: 師=シ(스승).

questions.stem: 今から行うインタビューの（　　　）は、授業に対する希望を聞くことです。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 2 is correct. It means 目的 (purpose).

Question Translation
「今から行うインタビューの（　　　）は、授業に対する希望を聞くことです。」→ The purpose of the interview we are about to conduct is to ask about students' wishes regarding the class.

Explanation
The sentence explains what the interview is for. That is the purpose, so 目的 fits naturally in the blank.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 影響 – Means influence, effect. This describes the result something has on something else, not the goal of the interview.
3. 様子 – Means appearance, state, situation. It describes how things look or seem, not the purpose.
4. 原因 – Means cause, reason (why something happened). It does not mean what you are trying to achieve.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
A の目的は B です is a common pattern meaning the purpose of A is B. Example: このプロジェクトの目的は売り上げを伸ばすことです.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「目的(もくてき)」 — 목적. 인터뷰의 목적은 ~을 묻는 것.
오답: 1 影響(영향), 3 様子(모습), 4 原因(원인)은 「~は…を聞くことです」(목적 설명) 흐름과 맞지 않음.
포인트: 「Nの目的は〜ことだ」.

questions.stem: 食事中、コップを（　　　）に落として割ってしまった。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 1 is correct. It means 床 (floor).

Question Translation
「食事中、コップを（　　　）に落として割ってしまった。」→ While eating, I dropped a glass onto the floor and broke it.

Explanation
When you drop a cup during a meal and it breaks, it naturally falls to the floor, so 床 is the correct word.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
2. 天井 – Means ceiling. Dropping something to the ceiling does not make sense.
3. 屋根 – Means roof. This is outside on top of a building, not where you eat.
4. 壁 – Means wall. A glass can hit a wall, but the most natural place to drop it onto during a meal is the floor.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
に落とす expresses the target place something is dropped onto: 床に落とす (drop on the floor), 川に落とす (drop into a river).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「床(ゆか)」 — 바닥. 컵을 바닥에 떨어뜨려 깨뜨림.
오답: 2 天井(천장), 3 屋根(지붕), 4 壁(벽)은 떨어진 컵이 닿는 자리로 부적절.
포인트: 落とす는 보통 床으로 향함.

questions.stem: 駅前に建設中の立派なマンションが、来月ついに（　　　）するらしい。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 2 is correct. It means 完成 (completion).

Question Translation
「駅前に建設中の立派なマンションが、来月ついに（　　　）するらしい。」→ The fine condominium being built in front of the station is apparently finally going to be completed next month.

Explanation
マンション is being built (建設中). The natural verb when a building is finished is 完成する (to be completed), so 完成 fits best.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 開場 – Means opening of a hall or venue (e.g. concert hall). It is not normally used for a building like マンション itself finishing construction.
3. 発展 – Means development, growth (often of a town or industry). It does not mean a single building is finished.
4. 進行 – Means progress, advancement (of work, disease etc.). It describes the ongoing process, not the moment of completion.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
建設中 (under construction) pairs naturally with 完成する (to be completed). Pattern: 建設中のビルが完成する.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「完成(かんせい)」 — 완성. 건설 중인 맨션이 완성된다.
오답: 1 開場(개장)은 행사장, 3 発展(발전), 4 進行(진행)은 건물이 끝났다는 의미가 약함.
포인트: 建設中→完成(자연스러운 한 쌍).

questions.stem: この店の料理は安くておいしいので、店の前にはいつも長い（　　　）ができている。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 2 is correct. It means 列 (line, queue).

Question Translation
「この店の料理は安くておいしいので、店の前にはいつも長い（　　　）ができている。」→ Because this restaurant's food is cheap and tasty, there is always a long line in front of the shop.

Explanation
People waiting to enter form a line, so 列 (queue) is the correct word.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 線 – Means line in the sense of a drawn line, route, or wire. It is not used for a queue of people.
3. 帯 – Means belt, band, zone. Not used for a line of people waiting.
4. 波 – Means wave (of water, or figurative wave). Does not describe people standing one behind another.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
列ができる means a line forms. You often see 行列ができる店 for a very popular restaurant with long lines.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「列(れつ)」 — 줄. 「長い列ができる」(긴 줄이 생기다).
오답: 1 線(선, 도형), 3 帯(띠), 4 波(파도)는 사람이 늘어선 것을 표현하지 못함.
포인트: 「列ができる」 관용 표현.

questions.stem: 最近太ったので、ズボンがきつくて、はくとすぐに（　　　）なってしまう。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 1 is correct. It means くるしく (painful, tight, uncomfortable).

Question Translation
「最近太ったので、ズボンがきつくて、はくとすぐに（　　　）なってしまう。」→ I have gained weight recently, so my pants are tight, and as soon as I put them on, they quickly become uncomfortable.

Explanation
Tight pants make you feel physically uncomfortable or in pain around the waist, so くるしくなる fits the situation.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
2. まぶしく – Means dazzling, blinding (because of light). This is used for light, not for pants.
3. きびしく – Means strict, severe. This is used for rules, people, or conditions, not physical tightness on the body.
4. あやしく – Means suspicious, strange. This is about how something seems, not how clothing feels.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
Adjective stem + くなる expresses a change of state: くるしい → くるしくなる (become painful/uncomfortable).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「苦(くる)しく」 — 괴롭다. 살이 쪄서 바지가 꽉 끼어 답답.
오답: 2 まぶしく(눈부시다), 3 きびしく(엄하다), 4 あやしく(수상하다)는 의미 불일치.
포인트: 「きつい→苦しい」 신체 감각.

questions.stem: この小説の中に（　　　）する人たちは、皆大きな夢を持っている。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 2 is correct. It means 登場 (appearance, to appear in a story or on stage).

Question Translation
「この小説の中に（　　　）する人たちは、皆大きな夢を持っている。」→ The people who appear in this novel all have big dreams.

Explanation
登場する is the standard verb meaning to appear in a story, novel, play, etc., so it fits perfectly here.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 滞在 – Means to stay (at a place), like staying in a hotel or city. It is not used for fictional characters appearing in a story.
3. 発生 – Means to occur, happen (often for accidents, events, disasters). It does not describe characters appearing.
4. 同席 – Means to be present together at a meeting or meal. It is not used for characters in a novel.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
小説に登場する人物 means characters who appear in a novel. 登場人物 is a fixed term for characters in a story.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「登場(とうじょう)」 — 등장. 소설 속에 등장하는 사람들.
오답: 1 滞在(체재), 3 発生(발생), 4 同席(동석)은 소설 등장과 맞지 않음.
포인트: 「小説に登場する」 정형 표현.

questions.stem: 薬を飲んでゆっくり休んだら（　　　）良くなったので、明日は会社に行けそうです。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 3 is correct. It means ずいぶん (considerably, a lot).

Question Translation
「薬を飲んでゆっくり休んだら（　　　）良くなったので、明日は会社に行けそうです。」→ After I took medicine and rested well, I got considerably better, so it looks like I can go to work tomorrow.

Explanation
The sentence describes a big improvement in condition, so ずいぶん (quite, very much) is the best choice.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. かならず – Means surely, without fail. It does not modify 良くなった in the sense of degree; it is about certainty.
2. なるべく – Means as much as possible. It is used before a verb like なるべく休む, not usually before 良くなった.
4. いつでも – Means anytime, always. It does not describe how much someone got better.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
ずいぶん is an adverb that shows a high degree: ずいぶん便利だ, ずいぶん変わった. It often comes before adjectives or past verbs.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「ずいぶん」 — 꽤, 상당히. 약 먹고 쉬어서 상태가 많이 좋아짐.
오답: 1 かならず(반드시), 2 なるべく(가능한 한), 4 いつでも(언제든)는 「良くなった」의 정도 부사로 맞지 않음.
포인트: 정도가 큰 변화 → ずいぶん.

questions.stem: 山口さんは、どんなときでも慌てないで（　　　）行動できる人だ。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 4 is correct. It means 落ちついて (calmly).

Question Translation
「山口さんは、どんなときでも慌てないで（　　　）行動できる人だ。」→ Mr. Yamaguchi is someone who can act calmly without panicking no matter what happens.

Explanation
慌てないで (without getting flustered) matches well with 落ちついて (in a calm way). Together they describe a person who stays calm in any situation.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 間に合って – Means in time, not late. It does not describe the manner of action.
2. 引受けて – Means to take on, accept a task. It does not talk about acting calmly.
3. 気になって – Means to be bothered by, worry about something. This is almost the opposite of being calm.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
慌てないで落ちついて行動する is a common expression meaning to act calmly without panicking.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「落(お)ち着(つ)いて」 — 침착하게. 「慌てないで」와 자연스러운 한 쌍.
오답: 1 間に合って(시간 맞춤), 2 引き受けて(맡다), 3 気になって(신경 쓰다)는 의미 불일치.
포인트: 「慌てない=落ち着く」.

questions.stem: パスポートを（　　　）するため、必要な書類を持って窓口へ行った。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 2 is correct. It means 申請 (application, to apply formally).

Question Translation
「パスポートを（　　　）するため、必要な書類を持って窓口へ行った。」→ In order to apply for a passport, I took the necessary documents to the counter.

Explanation
For official documents like passports, you 申請する (make a formal application), so 申請 is correct.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 応募 – Means to apply for something like a job, contest, or recruitment, not for issuing a passport.
3. 注文 – Means order (goods, food). You do not order a passport; you apply for it.
4. 期待 – Means expectation, hope. It is about feelings, not submitting forms.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
A を申請する is used for official permissions and documents such as パスポート, ビザ, 許可. The pattern A を申請するために B (in order to apply for A, I B) is also common.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「申請(しんせい)」 — 신청(공식). 여권을 신청.
오답: 1 応募(공모·응모), 3 注文(주문), 4 期待(기대)는 공문서 절차 표현으로 부적절.
포인트: 여권·비자 등은 申請.

questions.stem: 服にコーヒーがついて（　　　）になってしまい、何回も洗ったがきれいにならない。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 1 is correct. It means しみ (stain).

Question Translation
「服にコーヒーがついて（　　　）になってしまい、何回も洗ったがきれいにならない。」→ I got coffee on my clothes and it became a stain, and even after washing them many times it would not come out.

Explanation
Coffee on clothes leaves a mark that is hard to wash out, which is called a しみ (stain), so しみ is the correct choice.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
2. 傷 – Means wound, scratch, damage. It is used for injuries or physical damage, not for liquid marks.
3. かび – Means mold. Mold grows over time; it is different from a coffee stain.
4. 泡 – Means bubble or foam. That does not describe a mark left on clothes.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
しみがつく means to get stained, and しみが取れない means the stain will not come off.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「しみ」 — 얼룩. 커피 자국이 빠지지 않음.
오답: 2 傷(상처), 3 かび(곰팡이), 4 泡(거품)은 커피 자국과 다름.
포인트: 옷에 묻은 액체 자국 = しみ.

questions.stem: あの兄弟は双子みたいに顔が（　　　）で、区別できないね。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 3 is correct. It means そっくり (exactly alike).

Question Translation
「あの兄弟は双子みたいに顔が（　　　）で、区別できないね。」→ Those brothers look so alike in the face, like twins, that you cannot tell them apart.

Explanation
When two people look almost the same, you say そっくり. So そっくり fits perfectly with 双子みたいに and 区別できない.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. はっきり – Means clearly. It does not mean similar; it describes how clearly something can be seen.
2. うっかり – Means carelessly, by mistake. It does not describe appearance.
4. すっかり – Means completely, entirely. It is not used to say two people look alike.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
A は B にそっくりだ means A looks just like B. そっくり is often used for faces and voices.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「そっくり」 — 꼭 닮음. 쌍둥이처럼 얼굴이 닮아 구별 안 됨.
오답: 1 はっきり(분명), 2 うっかり(깜빡), 4 すっかり(완전히)는 외모 닮음을 못 표현.
포인트: 「双子みたい+そっくり」 정형 짝.

questions.stem: 前田さんも協力してください。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 3 is correct. It means 手伝って (help, assist).

Question Translation
「前田さんも協力してください。」→ Maeda, please cooperate and help us too.

Explanation
協力する means to cooperate, to help toward the same goal. In this context, it is close in meaning to 手伝う (to help), so 手伝って is the best paraphrase.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 急いで – Means hurry up. This is about speed, not about cooperating.
2. 決めて – Means decide. It does not mean help or work together.
4. がんばって – Means do your best. It focuses on effort, not on cooperating with others.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
協力する often implies working together toward a common goal, while 手伝う is more general help. Here, the nuance is please help us with this task, which matches 手伝ってください.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「手伝(てつだ)って」 — 돕다. 「協力する」(협력)와 가장 가까운 의미.
오답: 1 急いで(서두르다), 2 決めて(정하다), 4 がんばって(힘내다)는 협력의 패러프레이즈로 부족.
포인트: 協力する≒手伝う(같은 목표를 위해 돕다).

questions.stem: この仕事のスケジュールを教えてください。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 4 is correct. It means 予定 (schedule, plan).

Question Translation
「この仕事のスケジュールを教えてください。」→ Please tell me the schedule for this work.

Explanation
スケジュール is a loanword meaning schedule. The natural Japanese equivalent for a time plan is 予定, so 予定 is the correct paraphrase.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 規則 – Means rules, regulations. It does not mean schedule.
2. やり方 – Means way of doing, method. It talks about how to do the work, not when.
3. 結果 – Means result, outcome. It is what happens after, not the plan before.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
仕事のスケジュール and 仕事の予定 are both natural. 予定 focuses on the plan, while スケジュール feels a bit more businesslike or detailed.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「予定(よてい)」 — 예정. 「スケジュール」(외래어)와 동의.
오답: 1 規則(규칙), 2 やり方(방법), 3 結果(결과)는 일정과 다름.
포인트: スケジュール=予定.

questions.stem: 食事代は約1万円でした。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 1 is correct. It means だいたい1万円 (about 10,000 yen).

Question Translation
「食事代は約1万円でした。」→ The cost of the meal was about 10,000 yen.

Explanation
約 means about, approximately. だいたい1万円 expresses the same idea, so it is the correct paraphrase.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
2. ちょうど1万円 – Means exactly 10,000 yen, which is stronger than 約 and does not allow a range.
3. 1万円以上 – Means 10,000 yen or more, at least 10,000 yen, different nuance.
4. 1万円以下 – Means 10,000 yen or less, at most 10,000 yen, again different from about.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
約 and だいたい are both used for approximate numbers. 約 is a bit more formal, while だいたい is more conversational.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「だいたい1万円」 — 대략 1만 엔. 「約」(약)와 동의.
오답: 2 ちょうど1万円(정확히), 3 1만 엔 이상, 4 1만 엔 이하는 「約」(어림수)과 어긋남.
포인트: 約=だいたい.

questions.stem: 高橋さんにどなられた。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 1 is correct. It means 大声で怒られた (was scolded in a loud voice).

Question Translation
「高橋さんにどなられた。」→ Mr. Takahashi yelled at me angrily.

Explanation
どなられる means to be shouted at, usually in anger. This matches 大声で怒られた, being scolded in a loud voice.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
2. 小声で怒られた – Means was scolded in a low/quiet voice. どなられる implies loud shouting, so 小声 does not match.
3. 大声で笑われた – Means was laughed at in a loud voice. どなる is about anger, not laughter.
4. 小声で笑われた – Means was laughed at in a low voice. Again, どなる does not mean laugh.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
どなる is a strong verb meaning to yell or shout at someone in anger: 子どもをどなる, 部下をどなりつける.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「大声で怒(おこ)られた」 — 큰 소리로 야단맞다. 「どなる」(고함치다) = 화내며 큰 소리.
오답: 2 작은 소리로 야단(반대), 3·4 「笑う」(웃다)는 의미가 다름.
포인트: どなる=大声で怒る.

questions.stem: 中島さんはあらゆる質問に丁寧に答えた。
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 2 is correct. It means 全部の (all, every).

Question Translation
「中島さんはあらゆる質問に丁寧に答えた。」→ Ms. Nakajima answered every single question politely.

Explanation
あらゆる means all kinds of, every. It is close in meaning to 全部の, so 全部の質問に丁寧に答えた is a good paraphrase.

Why Other Options Are Incorrect
1. 一つの – Means one, a single. This is the opposite of あらゆる (all).
3. 難しい – Means difficult. It is an adjective and does not express all or every.
4. 簡単な – Means easy. Again, it does not express the meaning of all questions.

Grammar/Vocabulary Note
あらゆる is used before nouns to mean every possible X: あらゆる問題, あらゆる場合. It is a bit more formal than 全ての or すべての.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「全部(ぜんぶ)の」 — 모든. 「あらゆる」(온갖) = 모든 종류.
오답: 1 一つの(하나의)는 반대, 3 難しい(어려운)·4 簡単な(쉬운)는 의미가 다름.
포인트: あらゆる=全部の・すべての.

questions.stem: 減少
opts: わたしが勤めている高校では、図書室の利用者がかなり減少
今学期は熱心に勉強したのに、前の学期より成績が減少
この図は大きすぎるので、半分に減少
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 1 is correct.

Word Translation
減少（げんしょう） — decrease, reduction (in number, amount, or rate).

Correct Sentence Translation
「わたしが勤めている高校では、図書室の利用者がかなり減少している。」→ At the high school where I work, the number of users of the library has decreased considerably.

Explanation
減少する is used for numbers or amounts going down over time (population, users, sales, etc.). Here it refers to the number of people using the library, so 減少している is natural.

Other Options
2. 「セールで値段が減少したので、欲しかった服を数枚買った。」→ Because the price decreased in the sale, I bought several clothes I wanted.
   The idea is understandable, but for prices we normally say 値段が下がった or 値下げされた. 減少する is usually used for statistics or overall trends, so it sounds unnatural here.
3. 「今学期は熱心に勉強したのに、前の学期より成績が減少してしまった。」→ Although I studied hard this semester, my grades decreased compared to last semester.
   For grades or scores we usually say 成績が下がった or 成績が落ちた. 減少する sounds like a statistical report and is awkward in this personal context.
4. 「この図は大きすぎるので、半分に減少してコピーしてください。」→ This diagram is too big, so please decrease it to half and copy it.
   When we change the size of a picture or diagram, we use 縮小する (to reduce) or 半分に小さくする, not 減少する.

Usage Note
減少する is a formal verb often used with numbers like 人口 (population), 利用者数 (number of users), 売り上げ (sales), 事故件数 (number of accidents). It sounds like report or data language, not casual everyday expressions about a single price or picture size.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 — 「減少(げんしょう)」: 수·양이 줄다. 도서관 이용자가 상당히 감소.
오답: 2 가격(値段)은 「下がる」, 3 성적(成績)도 「下がる」, 4 그림 크기는 「小さくする」가 자연스러움.
포인트: 減少=수치·양의 감소.

questions.stem: 身につける
opts: 山下さんはたくさんの友人を身につけて
今回の留学では多くの知識を身につける
わたしは栄養を身につけて
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 2 is correct.

Word Translation
身につける — to acquire, learn (knowledge, skills, habits); also to wear (on one's body).

Correct Sentence Translation
「今回の留学では多くの知識を身につけることができたと思う。」→ I think that during this study abroad, I was able to acquire a lot of knowledge.

Explanation
身につける is commonly used for knowledge, skills, techniques, and habits that you acquire and make part of yourself. 多くの知識を身につける is a very natural expression.

Other Options
1. 「山下さんはたくさんの友人を身につけている。」→ Mr. Yamashita has acquired many friends.
   We do not say 友人を身につける. For friends we use 友人が多い or 友人を作る. 身につける does not fit with people.
3. 「わたしは栄養を身につけているので、とても健康だ。」→ I have acquired nutrition, so I am very healthy.
   栄養は身につけるとは言わず、栄養をとる or 栄養のある食事をしている is natural. 身につける is not used for nutrition.
4. 「毎朝、犬を身につけてこの公園を散歩している。」→ Every morning, I wear my dog and walk in this park.
   For animals you bring with you, you say 犬を連れて散歩する. 身につける is for things worn on the body (clothes, accessories) or for abstract things like skills, not for pets.

Usage Note
身につける has two main uses: 1) to put on or carry on your body (アクセサリーを身につける, ナイフを身につける), and 2) to acquire skills or knowledge (語学力を身につける, 礼儀を身につける). It is not used for people or food.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 — 「身(み)につける」: 지식·기술·습관을 익히다. 유학 중 많은 지식을 익힘.
오답: 1 친구는 「作る」, 3 영양은 「とる」, 4 개를 「身につける」는 불가능(개는 「連れる」).
포인트: 身につける=지식·기술의 습득.

questions.stem: 中古
opts: この会社では、働きはじめて3年目の社員は十分に中古
新しい冷蔵庫は高いので先輩から中古
高校を卒業して10年もたち、同級生たちとの友情も中古
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 3 is correct.

Word Translation
中古（ちゅうこ） — second-hand, used (not new).

Correct Sentence Translation
「新しい冷蔵庫は高いので先輩から中古をもらったが、問題なく使えそうだ。」→ A new refrigerator is expensive, so I got a used one from my senior, but it seems I can use it without any problems.

Explanation
中古 is normally used for used goods like cars, houses, appliances, and books. A used refrigerator from a senior is a typical example, so this sentence is natural.

Other Options
1. 「この会社では、働きはじめて3年目の社員は十分に中古として頼られている。」→ At this company, employees in their third year are relied on enough as used.
   For people with some experience we say 中堅, ベテラン, or 経験者. 中古 is not used for people.
2. 「この情報はもう中古なので、最新のニュースが知りたい。」→ This information is already used, so I want to know the latest news.
   Old or outdated information is described as 古い or 古くなった, not 中古. 中古 is for physical goods.
4. 「高校を卒業して10年もたち、同級生たちとの友情も中古になってしまった。」→ Ten years have passed since high school graduation, and my friendships with classmates have also become used.
   Friendships do not become 中古. You might say 薄くなった (thinned) or 昔ほどではなくなった, but 中古 is wrong for human relationships.

Usage Note
中古 is used for items that can be bought or sold as not new: 中古車 (used car), 中古マンション (pre-owned apartment), 中古本 (used book). For abstract things like information, feelings, or people, a different word is needed.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 — 「中古(ちゅうこ)」: 중고품. 선배에게서 중고 냉장고를 받음.
오답: 1 사원에게 中古는 불가, 2 정보는 「古い」, 4 우정도 中古 안 됨.
포인트: 中古=물건 한정(차·가전 등).

questions.stem: 断る
opts: みんなに反対されたが、わたしは自分の夢を断らな
誰もそのうわさを断ら
わたしは40歳のとき、勤めていた会社を断って
友人に映画に行こうと言われたが、用事があるので誘いを断った
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 4 is correct.

Word Translation
断る（ことわる） — to refuse, decline, turn down (an offer, invitation, request).

Correct Sentence Translation
「友人に映画に行こうと言われたが、用事があるので誘いを断った。」→ A friend asked me to go to the movies, but since I have things to do, I declined the invitation.

Explanation
断る is commonly used with 誘い (invitation), 申し出 (offer), 頼み (request), meaning to say no. 誘いを断った is a very natural phrase.

Other Options
1. 「みんなに反対されたが、わたしは自分の夢を断らなかった。」→ Everyone opposed it, but I did not refuse my dream.
   For dreams, we say 夢をあきらめなかった (did not give up my dream). 夢を断る is unnatural.
2. 「誰もそのうわさを断らないので、本当のことなのかもしれない。」→ Since nobody refuses that rumor, it might be true.
   For a rumor, we say 認める (admit) or 否定する (deny). うわさを断る is not used.
3. 「わたしは40歳のとき、勤めていた会社を断って、農業を始めた。」→ At 40, I refused the company I worked for and started farming.
   Leaving a company is 会社を辞める or 退職する. 会社を断る sounds like refusing a job offer, not quitting.

Usage Note
断る is used for refusing something that is offered or requested: 招待を断る, 面接を断る, 申し出を断る. For quitting a job or giving up a dream, different verbs like 辞める or あきらめる are used.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 — 「断(ことわ)る」: (권유·부탁을) 거절. 친구의 영화 권유를 거절.
오답: 1 꿈은 「あきらめる」, 2 소문은 「信じない」, 3 회사는 「辞める」.
포인트: 「誘いを断る」 정형 짝.

questions.stem: 受取る
opts: 父親は走ってきた子どもを両手で受取った
わたしの家では毎年ホームステイの学生を受取って
送ってくださった資料は、本日確かに受取り
隣にいた人が倒れそうになったので、腕を受取って
questions.expl:
Correct Answer
Answer 3 is correct.

Word Translation
受取る（うけとる） — to receive, accept; especially to receive something that is handed or sent to you. (Standard spelling is 受け取る.)

Correct Sentence Translation
「送ってくださった資料は、本日確かに受取りました。」→ I have certainly received the materials you sent today.

Explanation
受取る is naturally used when you receive documents, parcels, or money from someone. 資料を受取る is a standard business expression, so this sentence is correct.

Other Options
1. 「父親は走ってきた子どもを両手で受取った。」→ The father received his running child with both hands.
   For catching or stopping a falling person, we usually say 受け止めた or 抱きとめた. 受取った is mainly for receiving things, not for catching people.
2. 「わたしの家では毎年ホームステイの学生を受取っています。」→ At my house we receive homestay students every year.
   For welcoming or hosting people, the natural verb is 受け入れる or 受け入れている. 受取る is not used for accepting people to stay.
4. 「隣にいた人が倒れそうになったので、腕を受取ってあげた。」→ The person next to me was about to fall, so I received their arm.
   Again, we say 腕を支えてあげた (supported their arm) or 体を支えた. 受取る sounds like receiving an object, not supporting a person.

Usage Note
受取る/受け取る is commonly used with 荷物 (packages), 手紙 (letters), メール, 給料 (salary), and 書類 (documents). For catching or supporting people, verbs like 支える, 受け止める, or 抱きとめる are more appropriate.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 — 「受(う)け取る」: (보내온 것을) 받다. 자료를 받았다.
오답: 1 아이를 안다(「抱きとめる」), 2 학생을 「受け入れる」, 4 팔을 「支える」.
포인트: 受け取る=서류·물건의 수령.

questions.stem: 留学中、ときどき届く家族（　　　）手紙は、本当にうれしかった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4: からの is correct. It means 'from (someone)', used before a noun.

**Question Translation**
留学中、ときどき届く家族（　　　）手紙は、本当にうれしかった。
→ 'While I was studying abroad, the letters that sometimes arrived from my family made me really happy.'

**Explanation**
The pattern 「N1からのN2」 means 'N2 from N1'. Here we need 'letters from my family', so 「家族からの手紙」 is the only natural expression.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. と — 'and / with'.
→ 「家族と手紙」 would mean 'family and letters', which does not match the meaning.
2. から — 'from'.
→ 「家族から手紙」 is fine if the sentence is written that way, but the blank is directly before 手紙, so we need the form with の: からの.
3. との — 'with / between' (as in 「友達との会話」 'conversation with a friend').
→ 「家族との手紙」 is not natural Japanese; for 'letters from family' we normally say 「家族からの手紙」.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「NからのN」 is a common way to make 'N that comes from N'. For example: 「会社からの連絡」 (contact from the company), 「友人からのメール」 (an email from a friend).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「からの」 — ~로부터의(명사 수식). 「家族からの手紙」(가족이 보낸 편지).
오답: 1 と(と의 단독), 2 から는 명사 앞에 「の」가 필요, 3 との는 「と」 관계.
포인트: 「Nからの+명사」 형식.

questions.stem: （お知らせで）　東側の駐車場は現在工事中の（　　　）、南側の駐車場をご利用ください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2: ため is correct. Here it means 'because / due to'.

**Question Translation**
（お知らせで）東側の駐車場は現在工事中の（　　　）、南側の駐車場をご利用ください。
→ '(Notice) Because the east parking lot is currently under construction, please use the south parking lot.'

**Explanation**
The pattern 「Nのため」 means 'because of N' or 'due to N'. 「工事中のため」 = 'due to construction', which fits a formal notice.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. ほか — 'other, besides'.
→ 「工事中のほか」 would mean 'besides being under construction', which is unnatural here.
3. 途中 — 'on the way, in the middle of'.
→ 「工事中の途中」 is strange; normally we say 「工事の途中」. It also does not give a clear reason like 'because of'.
4. 一方 — 'on the other hand'.
→ This shows contrast between two things, not a reason. A notice needs a cause–result relation ('because it is under construction...'), so 一方 is wrong.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「Nのため、〜」 is a formal way to say 'because of N, ~', often used in announcements: 「雪のため、電車が遅れています」 ('Because of the snow, trains are delayed').
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「ため」 — ~때문에(이유). 「工事中のため」(공사 중이라).
오답: 1 ほか(이외), 3 途中(도중), 4 一方(한편)은 이유 표현이 아님.
포인트: 「Nのため」=원인·이유(격식).

questions.stem: （駅のホームで）妹「ねえ、トイレへ行ってきてもいい？」姉「うん。でも、（　　　）5分で電車が来ちゃうから、急いでね。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1: あと is correct. In this pattern it means 'in (time remaining)'.

**Question Translation**
姉: うん。でも、（　　　）5分で電車が来ちゃうから、急いでね。
→ 'Yeah. But the train will come in 5 minutes, so hurry, okay?'

**Explanation**
「あ と5分で」 is a fixed expression meaning 'in 5 more minutes / only 5 minutes left'. It matches the idea that the train is coming soon.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. まだ — 'still, yet'.
→ 「まだ5分で電車が来る」 is unnatural. We would say 「まだ5分ある」 ('there are still 5 minutes'), but that does not appear here.
3. だんだん — 'gradually'.
→ Used for gradual change (だんだん寒くなる, 'it gradually becomes cold'), not for time like 'in 5 minutes'.
4. 今にも — 'any moment now, at any second'.
→ Usually used with a verb in progressive form or ～そうだ: 「今にも雨が降りそうだ」. It does not combine with a specific time like '5 minutes'.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「あと＋時間＋で」 means 'in (time) from now': 「あと10分で始まります」 ('It will start in 10 minutes').
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「あと」 — (시간) ~만 남았다. 「あと5分で」(앞으로 5분이면).
오답: 2 まだ는 「まだ5分ある」 형식, 3 だんだん(점점), 4 今にも(당장이라도)는 어울리지 않음.
포인트: 「あと+시간で」=남은 시간 표현.

questions.stem: ハンバーグを作るのは難しいと思っていたが、（　　　）思ったより難しくなかった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3: 作ってみると is correct. It means 'when I actually tried making (it)'.

**Question Translation**
ハンバーグを作るのは難しいと思っていたが、（　　　）思ったより難しくなかった。
→ 'I had thought that making hamburger steak would be difficult, but when I actually tried making it, it was not as hard as I expected.'

**Explanation**
「Vてみると」 means 'when I try doing V, I find that...'. The sentence contrasts the speaker's expectation with the actual experience after trying to cook.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 作っているうちに — 'while I am making it / as I keep making it'.
→ This suggests something changes during the process, but here the key point is the result after trying once, not a change over time.
2. 作ったとしても — 'even if I (hypothetically) made it'.
→ This is hypothetical; it does not match the real experience that already happened.
4. 作った後で — 'after making it'.
→ 「作った後で思ったより難しくなかった」 is grammatically possible, but unnatural here. The standard pattern to express 'I tried it and found...' is 「作ってみると」.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「Vてみる」 is very common: 'try doing V and see'. Often followed by a discovery: 「食べてみると、おいしかった」 ('When I tried eating it, it was tasty').
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「作ってみると」 — 실제로 만들어 보니. 예상과 다른 발견.
오답: 1 作っているうちに(만드는 동안에), 2 作ったとしても(만든다 해도), 4 作った後で(만든 후에)는 「思ったより〜」(생각보다 ~)와 어울리지 않음.
포인트: 「Vてみると」=시도해보니 발견.

questions.stem: 中村「課長、昨日（　　　）お土産のチョコレート、とてもおいしかったです。ありがとうございました。」課長「ああ、それはよかった。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4: いただいた is correct. It is the humble form of もらう ('to receive').

**Question Translation**
中村: 課長、昨日（　　　）お土産のチョコレート、とてもおいしかったです。ありがとうございました。
→ 'Section chief, the souvenir chocolates I received from you yesterday were very delicious. Thank you very much.'

**Explanation**
「いただく」 is the humble version of もらう used when you receive something from someone of higher status. 「昨日いただいたお土産のチョコレート」 politely means 'the souvenir chocolates I received from you yesterday'.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. おりました — humble form of いる ('to be').
→ Used for existence ('I was there'), not for receiving something.
2. まいりました — humble form of 来る／行く ('to come / go').
→ Means 'I came / went', not 'received'.
3. なさった — honorific form of する ('to do').
→ Describes what the superior did, not what the speaker received. Here the focus is on the chocolates the speaker got.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
In honorific / humble language:
・あげる → 差し上げる (humble)
・くれる → 下さる (honorific)
・もらう → いただく (humble)
Here we talk about the speaker receiving, so いただく is appropriate.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「いただいた」 — 받은(겸양). 과장으로부터 받은 초콜릿.
오답: 1 おりました(겸양 「いる」)·2 まいりました(겸양 「行く・来る」)는 의미가 다름. 3 なさった(존경 「する」)는 화자 자신에게 못 씀.
포인트: もらう의 겸양어=いただく.

questions.stem: 初めて彼女に会ったとき、「なんてきれいな人（　　　）」と思った。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1: なんだろう is correct.

**Question Translation**
初めて彼女に会ったとき、「なんてきれいな人（　　　）」と思った。
→ 'When I first met her, I thought, "What a beautiful person she is!"'

**Explanation**
The pattern 「なんて〜なんだろう」 expresses a strong emotional reaction ('what a ~!', 'so ~!'). So 「なんてきれいな人なんだろう」 is a natural exclamation of admiration.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. だった — 'was'.
→ 「なんてきれいな人だった」 sounds like looking back after some time, not a direct first impression at that moment.
3. みたいだ — 'seems like, looks like'.
→ 「なんてきれいな人みたいだ」 is unnatural; with なんて, we normally use 〜なんだ／〜なんだろう.
4. だったのか — 'was it that...'.
→ 「なんてきれいな人だったのか」 sounds like a realized fact later on, not a simple exclamation at first meeting.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「なんて〜なんだ／なんだろう」 is often used for strong feeling:
・なんてすてきな景色なんだろう。('What a beautiful view!')
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「なんだろう」 — 감탄. 「なんて〜なんだろう」(얼마나 ~인지!).
오답: 2 だった(과거 단정), 3 みたいだ(추측), 4 だったのか(과거 의문)는 감탄 구조와 안 맞음.
포인트: 「なんて+형용/명사+(な)んだろう」=강한 감탄.

questions.stem: （電話で）店員「お電話ありがとうございます。さくら美容院（　　　）。」客「すみません、今日予約したいんですが。」店員「はい、ありがとうございます。何時ごろがよろしいでしょうか。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2: でございます is correct. It is the polite form of です.

**Question Translation**
（電話で）店員: お電話ありがとうございます。さくら美容院（　　　）。
→ '(On the phone) Clerk: Thank you for calling. This is Sakura Beauty Salon.'

**Explanation**
On the phone in polite business Japanese, shops normally introduce themselves with 「〜でございます」 ('This is ~'). So 「さくら美容院でございます」 is the standard expression.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. でいらっしゃいます — honorific 'to be'.
→ Used about the customer or a third person, not usually for the speaker's own shop name in this pattern.
3. と申し上げます — humble 'to say (that I am)'.
→ Used when giving your own name: 「山田と申し上げます」. It sounds strange after a shop name.
4. とおっしゃいます — honorific 'to be called'.
→ Used for someone else's name (e.g. お名前はなんとおっしゃいますか), not for the speaker's shop.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
Phone formulas:
・会社名＋でございます — 'This is (company)'.
・（私、）山田と申します — 'My name is Yamada.'
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「でございます」 — です의 정중형(접객). 「さくら美容院でございます」.
오답: 1 でいらっしゃいます(상대에게 쓰는 존경), 3 と申し上げます(말씀드리다, 사람 이름 한정), 4 とおっしゃいます(상대 발화).
포인트: 자기 소속·이름 정중 표명=でございます.

questions.stem: 中西「川村さん、昨日のダンス教室の見学、どうだった？入るの？」 川村「ううん、あまり（　　　）から、入らないことにしたよ。」
opts: おもしろかったはずがない
おもしろくなければならなかった
おもしろそうじゃなかった
おもしろくなかっただろう
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3: おもしろそうじゃなかった is correct. It means 'didn't look interesting'.

**Question Translation**
川村: ううん、あまり（　　　）から、入らないことにしたよ。
→ 'Nah, since it didn’t really look interesting, I decided not to join.'

**Explanation**
「〜そうだ」 describes appearance or impression. 「おもしろそうじゃなかった」 = 'did not seem interesting', which matches the reason for not joining after observing the class.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. おもしろかったはずがない — 'there’s no way it was interesting'.
→ This denies a fact, and sounds like judging something already known, not 'it didn’t look interesting'.
2. おもしろくなければならなかった — 'it had to be interesting'.
→ Means it was required to be interesting; this does not fit the speaker’s reason.
4. おもしろくなかっただろう — 'it probably wasn’t interesting'.
→ This sounds like guessing afterward, not talking about your own impression while watching.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「い形容詞そうだ」 = 'looks/seems ~'. Negative: 「おもしろそうじゃない」 (doesn't look interesting), past negative: 「おもしろそうじゃなかった」.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「おもしろそうじゃなかった」 — 재미있어 보이지 않았다. 가입 안 한 이유.
오답: 1 「はずがない」(~일 리 없다)는 불일치, 2 「なければならなかった」(~해야 했다)는 의미 반대, 4 「なかっただろう」(추측)는 단정 부족.
포인트: 「〜そうだ」=외관·인상.

questions.stem: 旅行の楽しみの一つは、そこ（　　　）食べられない料理を食べることだ。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1: でしか is correct. It means 'only at (there)'.

**Question Translation**
旅行の楽しみの一つは、そこ（　　　）食べられない料理を食べることだ。
→ 'One of the pleasures of traveling is eating dishes that you can eat only there.'

**Explanation**
「NでしかVない」 means 'can only V at/in N'. So 「そこでしか食べられない料理」 naturally means 'food that you can eat only there'.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. だけの — 'only, just (of)'.
→ 「そこだけの食べられない料理」 is ungrammatical. We would need something like 「そこだけの料理」, but that still doesn’t match the pattern before 食べられない.
3. だけ — 'only'.
→ 「そこだけ食べられない料理」 sounds like 'dishes you cannot eat only there', which is confusing and wrong.
4. での — 'at, in (used to modify a noun)'.
→ 「そこでの食べられない料理」 is not natural; での is used before a noun like 「そこでの経験」 ('experience there').

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「Nでしか〜ない」 is a very common pattern: 「日本でしか買えないお土産」 ('souvenirs you can buy only in Japan').
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「でしか」 — ~에서만(한정). 「そこでしか食べられない」(거기서만 먹을 수 있다).
오답: 2 だけの·3 だけ는 「食べられない」(부정)과 결합 부자연, 4 での는 한정 의미 없음.
포인트: 「NでしかVない」=N에서만 V할 수 있다.

questions.stem: りんごはそのまま食べるのもおいしいが、ジャム（　　　）おいしい。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2: にしても is correct. Here it means 'when you make it into ~ as well'.

**Question Translation**
りんごはそのまま食べるのもおいしいが、ジャム（　　　）おいしい。
→ 'Apples are tasty eaten just as they are, but they’re also delicious when you make them into jam.'

**Explanation**
「Nにしても」 after a noun here means 'even when made into N / also as N'. So 「ジャムにしてもおいしい」 is natural: 'they’re also good as jam'.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. にするのに — 'in order to make into ~ / for making into ~'.
→ 「ジャムにするのにおいしい」 is unnatural; we don’t say fruit is 'delicious for making into jam' like this.
3. になると — 'when it becomes ~'.
→ 「ジャムになるとおいしい」 is grammatically possible but sounds odd about apples; the exam expects the very common pattern にしても here.
4. になって — 'becoming ~ and...'.
→ 「ジャムになっておいしい」 is unnatural phrasing; we would normally say 「ジャムにしてもおいしい」.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「Nにしても〜」 can mean 'even if N' or 'also when N', but in this kind of listing, it often means 'also as N': 「焼いてもおいしいし、スープにしてもおいしい」.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「にしても」 — ~으로 해도(만들어도). 잼으로 만들어도 맛있다.
오답: 1 にするのに(만드는데), 3 になると(~이 되면), 4 になって(~이 되어)는 「(가공 결과)도 맛있다」 의미를 못 살림.
포인트: 「Nにしても」=N으로 해도 마찬가지.

questions.stem: 私はたまに電気とテレビを（　　　）朝まで寝てしまうことがある。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4: つけたまま is correct. It means 'leaving (them) turned on'.

**Question Translation**
私はたまに電気とテレビを（　　　）朝まで寝てしまうことがある。
→ 'Sometimes I end up sleeping until morning with the lights and TV left on.'

**Explanation**
「Vたまま」 expresses 'leaving something in the state resulting from V'. 「つけたまま」 = 'leaving (them) turned on', which matches the idea that the person fell asleep without turning them off.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. ついている間 — 'while they are on'.
→ Focuses on 'while they are on', not on the idea of 'left on unintentionally'.
2. つけている間 — 'while (I) am turning them on / keeping them on'.
→ Sounds like the person is intentionally keeping them on while awake, not that they fell asleep.
3. ついたまま — 'remaining on' (intransitive つく).
→ Grammatically possible but less natural here; we usually talk about our own action of leaving them on with つけたまま.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「Vたまま」 = 'leave something as it is':
・靴をはいたまま部屋に入る ('enter the room with shoes still on').
・テレビをつけたまま寝る ('sleep with the TV left on').
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「つけたまま」 — 켠 채. 전등·TV는 타동사 「つける」+「たまま」.
오답: 1·3은 자동사 「ついている/ついた」로 전등·TV를 직접 목적어로 못 받음(を 충돌). 2 つけている間는 「~하는 동안 잤다」 의미가 어색.
포인트: 「Vたまま」=V한 상태 유지.

questions.stem: 先月買った靴は、（　　　）足が疲れないので気に入っている。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1: 歩きやすくて is correct. It means 'easy to walk in, so...'.

**Question Translation**
先月買った靴は、（　　　）足が疲れないので気に入っている。
→ 'I like the shoes I bought last month because they’re easy to walk in and my feet don’t get tired.'

**Explanation**
「Vやすい」 means 'easy to V'. 「歩きやすくて」 connects the reason: 'because they are easy to walk in, (my feet don’t get tired)'.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. 歩きすぎて — 'because I walk too much'.
→ This would normally cause tired feet, not prevent them from getting tired.
3. 歩きやすかったら — 'if they are easy to walk in'.
→ This is a hypothetical 'if'; the sentence states a real reason, not a condition.
4. 歩きすぎたら — 'if I walk too much'.
→ Again this suggests tiredness, the opposite of the meaning here.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
い形容詞＋くて and 「Vやすい」:
・この靴は軽くて歩きやすいです ('These shoes are light and easy to walk in').
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「歩(ある)きやすくて」 — 걷기 편해서. 발이 안 피곤한 이유.
오답: 2 歩きすぎて(너무 걸어서), 3 やすかったら(가정), 4 すぎたら(가정)는 「疲れない」(안 피곤하다)의 이유로 맞지 않음.
포인트: 「Vやすい」+て=이유.

questions.stem: （電話で）父「はい、山下です。」 息子「あ、もしもし、お父さん？今、お母さん、家にいる？」父「ああ、いるよ。ちょうど仕事から（　　　）よ。」
opts: 帰ってきたことがある
帰っていったことがある
帰っていったところだ
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3: 帰ってきたところだ is correct. It means 'has just come home'.

**Question Translation**
父: ああ、いるよ。ちょうど仕事から（　　　）よ。
→ 'Yeah, she’s here. She has just come home from work.'

**Explanation**
「Vたところだ」 means 'have just V-ed'. 「仕事から帰ってきたところだ」 shows that the mother has just arrived home right now.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 帰ってきたことがある — 'has come home (at some time in the past)'.
→ Describes experience, not something that just happened now.
2. 帰っていったことがある — 'has gone back (away) at some time'.
→ Also experience, and いく suggests going away, not coming home.
4. 帰っていったところだ — 'has just gone back (left)'.
→ This would mean she just went away, opposite of the meaning.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「Vたところだ」 = 'just did V':
・今帰ってきたところだ ('I’ve just come home').
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「帰(かえ)ってきたところだ」 — 막 돌아왔다. 「ちょうど」와 호응.
오답: 1 ことがある(경험), 2·4 「帰っていった」(가버렸다)는 화자 쪽 시점과 안 맞음.
포인트: 「Vたところだ」=막 ~했다(직후).

questions.stem: 課長「山田さん、資料の整理をやってもらえますか。来週の金曜 ______ ______ ★ ______ 、できるときにお願いします。」
questions.expl:
**Answer (★ Position)**
Answer 3: いい

**Correct Order**
4 → 2 → 3 → 1  
📌 まで → で → いい → ですから

**Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown**
④ まで — 'until, by (time)'; sets the deadline: 来週の金曜まで ('until next Friday').
② で — 'by, as'; here it makes までで, meaning 'as long as it is by (that time)'.
③ いい — 'is fine'; indicates that the deadline is not strict as long as it’s met.
① ですから — 'so / therefore'; politely connects the time condition with the request.

**Reconstructed Sentence**
課長: 「山田さん、資料の整理をやってもらえますか。来週の金曜**まででいいですから、**できるときにお願いします。」
→ 'Yamada, could you organize these materials for me? Anytime is fine as long as it’s done by next Friday, so please do it when you have time.'

**Why This Order Works**
1. 「まで」 sets the final date (next Friday).
2. 「までで」 means 'as long as it’s up to that time'.
3. 「いい」 tells that this condition is acceptable (it doesn’t have to be earlier).
4. 「ですから」 connects the relaxed deadline with the follow-up request 「できるときにお願いします」.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 (★자리=세 번째 「いい」). 올바른 순서: まで→で→いい→ですから.
포인트: 「Nまでで(은 그때까지)+いい(괜찮다)+ですから(이니까)」 구성. 「될 수 있을 때 해 달라」는 유연한 마감 표현.

questions.stem: 私は、もし自分が ______ ______ ★ ______看護師の仕事をしています。
questions.expl:
**Answer (★ Position)**
Answer 2: ということを

**Correct Order**
3 → 4 → 2 → 1  
📌 患者だったら → どうしてほしいか → ということを → 考えながら

**Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown**
③ 患者だったら — 'if I were the patient'; sets up an imaginary situation.
④ どうしてほしいか — 'what I would want (someone) to do'; expresses the patient’s wish.
② ということを — nominalises the idea into 'the fact/idea that...'; joins the clause to a noun phrase.
① 考えながら — 'while thinking'; shows that the action is done while keeping this idea in mind.

**Reconstructed Sentence**
私は、もし自分が**患者だったらどうしてほしいかということを考えながら、**看護師の仕事をしています。
→ 'In my work as a nurse, I always work while thinking, "If I were the patient, what would I want (the nurse) to do?"'

**Why This Order Works**
1. 「患者だったら」 introduces the conditional 'if I were the patient'.
2. 「どうしてほしいか」 completes the embedded question 'what I would want (to be done)'.
3. 「ということを」 turns that whole clause into a thing/idea that can be 'thought about'.
4. 「考えながら」 describes how the speaker does their job, keeping that idea in mind.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 (★자리=세 번째 「ということを」). 올바른 순서: 患者だったら→どうしてほしいか→ということを→考えながら.
포인트: 「もし〜だったら〜か」(가정+의문) + 「ということを考える」(생각하다) 구조.

questions.stem: 私はエアコンの風が好きではないので、夏の夜、暑くて ______ ______ ★ ______ 、自分の部屋ではエアコンを使いません。
questions.expl:
**Answer (★ Position)**
Answer 2: とき

**Correct Order**
1 → 3 → 2 → 4  
📌 どうしても → 寝られない → とき → 以外は

**Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown**
① どうしても — 'no matter what, absolutely'; emphasises a strong degree.
③ 寝られない — 'cannot sleep'; describes the problem.
② とき — 'when'; makes the time clause 'when I just can’t sleep'.
④ 以外は — 'except for'; marks that this is the only exception.

**Reconstructed Sentence**
私はエアコンの風が好きではないので、夏の夜、暑くて**どうしても寝られないとき以外は、**自分の部屋ではエアコンを使いません。
→ 'I don’t like air conditioner wind, so on summer nights, except when it’s so hot that I absolutely can’t sleep, I don’t use the air conditioner in my room.'

**Why This Order Works**
1. 「どうしても」 strengthens the inability to sleep.
2. 「寝られないとき」 forms 'when I cannot sleep'.
3. 「以外は」 marks that only in that case is the rule different; otherwise, the speaker doesn’t use the air conditioner.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 (★자리=세 번째 「とき」). 올바른 순서: どうしても→寝られない→とき→以外は.
포인트: 「どうしても寝られないとき以外は」(아무리 해도 못 잘 때 외에는)=한정.

questions.stem: 最近、田中さんは元気がない。心配だが、何が  ______ ______ ★ ______ してあげられない。
questions.expl:
**Answer (★ Position)**
Answer 4: 答えてくれないので

**Correct Order**
2 → 1 → 4 → 3  
📌 あったのか → 聞いても → 答えてくれないので → 何も

**Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown**
② あったのか — 'what happened'; completes 'what (has) happened?'.
① 聞いても — 'even if I ask'; introduces a concessive condition.
④ 答えてくれないので — 'doesn’t answer me, so...'; gives the reason for the next clause.
③ 何も — 'anything, nothing (in a negative sentence)'; will modify してあげられない.

**Reconstructed Sentence**
最近、田中さんは元気がない。心配だが、何が**あったのか聞いても答えてくれないので、何も**してあげられない。
→ 'Recently Tanaka doesn’t seem well. I’m worried, but even if I ask what happened, he doesn’t tell me, so I can’t do anything for him.'

**Why This Order Works**
1. 「何があったのか」 is the content of the question: 'what happened?'.
2. 「聞いても」 marks 'even if I ask'.
3. 「答えてくれないので」 gives the reason — he won’t answer.
4. 「何もしてあげられない」 concludes that the speaker 'can’t do anything (for him)'.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 (★자리=세 번째 「答えてくれないので」). 올바른 순서: あったのか→聞いても→答えてくれないので→何も.
포인트: 「何が(あったのか)聞いても答えてくれないので、何も(してあげられない)」=무엇이 있었는지 물어도 대답이 없어 아무것도 해줄 수 없다.

questions.stem: この辺りは自然が多く、いつかこういうところに住んでみたいと思うが、近くに買い物できる場所がないので、______ ______ ★ ______  のは大変そうだ。
questions.expl:
**Answer (★ Position)**
Answer 1: 私には

**Correct Order**
4 → 2 → 1 → 3  
📌 車の運転が → できない → 私には → 生活する

**Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown**
④ 車の運転が — 'driving a car'; subject of できない.
② できない — 'cannot do'; states the inability to drive.
① 私には — 'for me'; marks the person for whom this is difficult.
③ 生活する — 'to live'; the action described as difficult.

**Reconstructed Sentence**
この辺りは自然が多く、いつかこういうところに住んでみたいと思うが、近くに買い物できる場所がないので、**車の運転ができない私には生活する**のは大変そうだ。
→ 'This area has a lot of nature and I’d like to live in a place like this someday, but since there’s nowhere nearby to shop, it seems that living here would be hard for me, because I can’t drive.'

**Why This Order Works**
1. 「車の運転ができない」 states the condition 'I can’t drive'.
2. 「私には」 marks that for this person in particular, it’s hard.
3. 「生活するのは大変そうだ」 means 'living (here) seems difficult', logically following from not being able to drive in a place without shops nearby.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 (★자리=세 번째 「私には」). 올바른 순서: 車の運転が→できない→私には→生活する.
포인트: 「車の運転ができない私には、生活するのは大変そうだ」=운전을 못 하는 나로서는 생활하기 힘들 듯.

questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3: それでも is correct. It means 'even so, nevertheless'.

**Question Translation (relevant part)**
いつも大勢で行くから、自分一人がずっと歌うということはできません。[19]、カラオケはパーティーみたいで楽しいと思っていました。
→ 'Since we always go in a big group, I can’t be the only one singing the whole time. Even so, I thought karaoke was fun, like a party.'

**Explanation**
The first sentence says there is a limitation (you can’t sing all the time), but the writer still feels that karaoke is fun. This contrast 'even though there is a limitation, it’s still fun' is expressed naturally with それでも.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. そのうえ — 'on top of that, furthermore'.
→ Adds another positive point; it doesn’t show contrast between a limitation and a positive feeling.
2. 特に — 'especially'.
→ Would make the sentence 'especially, I thought karaoke was like a party', which doesn’t connect logically with the previous limitation.
4. また — 'also, again'.
→ Simply adds another sentence, but here we need 'in spite of that', not 'also'.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
Text connectors:
・それでも — 'even so, nevertheless' (contrast with the previous sentence).
・そのうえ — 'moreover, in addition' (adds another similar point).
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「それでも」 — 그래도. 혼자 길게 못 부르지만, 그래도 파티 같아서 즐거웠다.
오답: 1 そのうえ(게다가), 2 特に(특히), 4 また(또한)는 역접의 의미를 못 살림.
포인트: 앞 문장과의 역접 → それでも.

opts: 思いつづけたようです
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1: 思っていたからです is correct. It means 'because I had thought (so)'.

**Question Translation (relevant part)**
カラオケは人と一緒に楽しむものだと[20]。
→ 'It was because I had thought that karaoke was something to be enjoyed together with other people.'

**Explanation**
This sentence explains why the writer was very surprised to see a 'solo karaoke' shop. The reason is: they had believed karaoke was for enjoying with others. 「〜と思っていたからです」 is the natural way to say 'it is because I had thought that ~'.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. 思っていたはずです — 'I must have been thinking so'.
→ This sounds like guessing about one’s own past thoughts; it does not clearly state the reason.
3. 思いつづけたのです — 'I kept on thinking so'.
→ Emphasises continuation, but does not fit the explanatory 'because' that the context needs.
4. 思いつづけたようです — 'it seems I kept on thinking so'.
→ Adds both continuation and uncertainty; unnatural when clearly explaining one’s own reason.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「〜からです」 is a very common way in writing to explain a reason: 'It is because ~'.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「思っていたからです」 — ~라고 생각하고 있었기 때문이다. 놀란 이유 설명.
오답: 2 はずです(당위), 3 思いつづけたのです(계속 생각했다), 4 ようです(추측)는 이유 표현으로 부족.
포인트: 「〜からです」=이유 설명.

opts: 行ってみることになっています
行ってみることにしました
行かせてくれたことです
行かせることができました
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2: 行ってみることにしました is correct. It means 'I decided to try going'.

**Question Translation (relevant part)**
一人で行ってもいいとわかって、私も一人で[21]。
→ 'Once I knew it was okay to go alone, I also decided to try going alone.'

**Explanation**
After learning that going alone is acceptable, the natural next step is 'so I decided to try going alone myself'. This idea is expressed by 「行ってみることにしました」 (I decided to try going).

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 行ってみることになっています — 'it is arranged that I will try going'.
→ Sounds like a fixed plan or rule decided by someone else; doesn’t match a personal decision.
3. 行かせてくれたことです — 'it is that (someone) let me go'.
→ Describes permission in the past; here there is no other person granting permission.
4. 行かせることができました — 'I was able to make (someone) go'.
→ Means the speaker made someone else go, which doesn’t fit the story.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「Vることにしました」 = 'I decided to do V'. Adding みる makes it 'decide to try doing V'.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「行ってみることにしました」 — 가보기로 했다. 본인의 결정.
오답: 1 ことになっています(정해져 있다), 3 行かせてくれた(허락 받음), 4 行かせることができました(시키다 가능)는 자기 결정 표현이 아님.
포인트: 「Vことにする」=스스로 결정.

questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 1: 部屋 is correct. It simply means 'the room'.

**Question Translation (relevant part)**
そして、[22]に入り、まだ上手に歌えない日本語の歌を練習したり、好きな歌を繰り返し歌ったりしました。
→ 'Then I went into the room and practiced Japanese songs I still couldn’t sing well, and sang my favorite songs over and over.'

**Explanation**
At a karaoke place, after writing your name at reception, the natural thing is to 'go into the room' you are given. There is no need to point to a specific type of room; simply 部屋 is correct.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
2. そんな部屋 — 'that kind of room'.
→ Refers to a room just described; but there has been no description of the room yet.
3. こんな部屋 — 'this kind of room'.
→ Used when describing a room currently being shown or described in detail; not the case here.
4. あんな部屋 — 'that (distant) kind of room'.
→ Refers to a room far away in space or memory; again, not appropriate here.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
Karaoke vocabulary: 「受付で名前を書いて、部屋に入る」 — 'write your name at reception and enter the room'.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「部屋(へや)」 — 방. 접수 후 들어가는 곳.
오답: 2 そんな·3 こんな·4 あんな(지시 연체사)는 앞에 가리킬 대상이 없어 부자연.
포인트: 처음 언급하는 대상 → 지시어 없이 「部屋」.

questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4: でも is correct. Here it means 'even (alone)'.

**Question Translation (relevant part)**
カラオケは一人[23]楽しかったです。
→ 'Karaoke was fun even by myself.'

**Explanation**
「一人でも」 means 'even alone / even when I am alone'. This matches the writer’s point: karaoke is fun not only with many people but also alone.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. だけ — 'only'.
→ 「一人だけ楽しかったです」 would mean 'only I had fun', which changes the meaning.
2. より — 'than'.
→ Needs a comparison (AよりB); here there is no comparison phrase.
3. なら — 'if, as for'.
→ 「一人なら楽しかったです」 is unnatural; we would normally say 「一人でも楽しかったです」 to mean 'it was fun even alone'.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
「Nでも」 after a noun often means 'even N':
・子どもでも分かります ('Even children can understand it').
・一人でも大丈夫です ('It’s okay even alone').
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「でも」 — ~でも(~조차도/~도). 「一人でも」(혼자라도).
오답: 1 だけ(만), 2 より(보다), 3 なら(라면)는 「혼자라도 즐거웠다」 의미를 못 살림.
포인트: 「Nでも」=N라도(양보·예시).

questions.stem: どうして姉は子どもにひらがなの名前をつけたのか。
opts: 小さいころから自分で書けるから
優しい感じがするから
読み間違えられることがないから
覚えてもらいやすいから
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It means: Because it gives a gentle feeling.

**Question Translation**
どうして姉は子どもにひらがなの名前をつけたのか。
→ Why did my older sister give her child a name written in hiragana?

**Explanation**
In the passage the sister clearly says that, if the name is written in hiragana, it gives a gentle, soft impression: ひらがななら、優しい感じがするから. That is her direct reason for choosing a hiragana-only name, so option 2 matches the text.

The narrator later adds their own thoughts about advantages such as no reading mistakes and being easy to remember, but those are not the sister’s stated reason.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 「小さいころから自分で書けるから」= Because the child can write it by herself from a young age.
→ This is the narrator’s guess (そうしたのかと思って) before asking the sister. It is not the actual reason she gives.

3. 「読み間違えられることがないから」= Because it will not be read incorrectly.
→ This is mentioned as one good point the narrator thinks of (良い点もあるだろう), not as the sister’s motivation.

4. 「覚えてもらいやすいから」= Because people can easily remember it.
→ Also only listed as another possible advantage, not as the stated reason for choosing the name.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
・〜から: a common way to give a reason, meaning because or since.
・感じがする: to feel like, to give the impression of. 優しい感じがする = it feels gentle / gives a gentle impression.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 — 부드러운 느낌을 주기 때문에.
근거: 본문에 「ひらがななら、優しい感じがするから」(히라가나라면 부드러운 느낌이 들기 때문에)라고 언니가 답함.
오답: 1 작을 때부터 자기가 쓸 수 있다는 점은 필자의 추측이었고 언니의 답이 아님. 3·4는 「~という良い点もあるだろう」로 필자가 덧붙인 부가 이점일 뿐 본 이유는 아님.

questions.stem: まり子さんは、ラナさんに来週の金曜日、どうしてほしいと言っているか。
opts: 駅の改札口で、自分を待っていてほしい。
コンサート会場に直接行って、外で自分を待っていてほしい。
自分を待たないで、先にコンサート会場に入っていてほしい。
コンサートに遅れそうなときは、自分にメールしてほしい。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 3 is correct. It means: She wants Rana to go in first without waiting for her.

**Question Translation**
まり子さんは、ラナさんに来週の金曜日、どうしてほしいと言っているか。
→ What is Mariko asking Rana to do next Friday?

**Explanation**
Mariko writes that this time Rana should go directly to the concert hall and go inside first: 今回は直接会場に行って、先に中に入っていてくれませんか. This shows she does not want Rana to wait outside or at the station, but to enter the venue without her if Mariko is late.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 「駅の改札口で、自分を待っていてほしい。」= She wants her to wait for her at the station ticket gate.
→ This is what they did the previous time (この前のコンサートのとき), and Mariko says this time she wants to do something different.

2. 「コンサート会場に直接行って、外で自分を待っていてほしい。」= She wants her to go directly to the concert hall and wait outside for her.
→ Mariko explicitly asks Rana to go directly to the venue and go inside first (先に中に入っていて), not wait outside.

4. 「コンサートに遅れそうなときは、自分にメールしてほしい。」= She wants Rana to email her if Rana might be late for the concert.
→ It is Mariko who says she will email Rana if she herself seems likely to be late (遅れそうだったら、メールしますね). The direction is the opposite.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
・〜ていてくれませんか: a polite request meaning please be doing X (for me). Here 中に入っていてくれませんか softens the request and sounds considerate.
・直接: directly, without going through another place.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 — 자기를 기다리지 말고 먼저 콘서트장에 들어가 있어 주길 바람.
근거: 「今回は直接会場に行って、先に中に入っていてくれませんか」(이번엔 직접 회장에 가서 먼저 들어가 있어 줘).
오답: 1 개찰구 만남은 지난번 방식, 2 밖에서 기다리라는 말은 없음, 4 메일은 늦을 것 같을 때 자신이 보낸다고 함.

questions.stem: 間違いとあるが、何が間違いか。
opts: コピー用紙１箱を送ってしまったこと
コピー用紙１箱を送らなかったこと
のりを５本送ってしまったこと
のりを３本しか送らなかったこと
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It means: They sent only three sticks of glue.

**Question Translation**
間違いとあるが、何が間違いか。
→ It says there was a mistake. What was the mistake?

**Explanation**
The email says the customer ordered 1 box of copy paper and 5 sticks of glue, but they only sent 3 sticks: のりは３本しかお送りしておりませんでした. So the mistake was not sending the full 5, but only 3. That matches option 4.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 「コピー用紙１箱を送ってしまったこと」= That they (wrongly) sent one box of copy paper.
→ Copy paper 1 box was ordered and correctly sent. This is not the mistake.

2. 「コピー用紙１箱を送らなかったこと」= That they did not send one box of copy paper.
→ The mail says they did send the copy paper. The problem is with the glue only.

3. 「のりを５本送ってしまったこと」= That they (wrongly) sent 5 sticks of glue.
→ 5 sticks was the correct quantity in the order, so sending 5 would not be a mistake. They actually sent only 3.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
・〜しか〜ない: only X and no more than that. のりは３本しかお送りしておりませんでした = we sent only 3 sticks of glue.
・お世話になっております and 申し訳ありません: set phrases in polite business emails.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 — 풀(のり)을 3개만 보낸 것.
근거: 주문은 복사용지 1박스·풀 5개였지만 「のりは３本しかお送りしておりませんでした」(풀은 3개밖에 보내지 않았습니다).
오답: 1·2 복사용지 1박스는 정상 발송, 3 풀 5개는 주문 수량 자체.

questions.stem: 「私」が最も言いたいことは何か。
opts: 日本では、田舎をゆっくり走る鉄道の旅を多くの人に楽しんでもらいたい。
日本では、かなりの田舎に住んでいても、鉄道が通っているので便利だ。
日本では、自動車の旅も鉄道の旅も楽しめるようになったので、うれしい。
日本では、鉄道が自動車より前に移動手段として広く使われるようになってよかった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 4 is correct. It means: In Japan it is good that railways came to be used widely before cars as a means of transportation.

**Question Translation**
「私」が最も言いたいことは何か。
→ What is the main thing that I want to say?

**Explanation**
The writer explains that even in quite rural areas, railways exist in Japan because railways became a common means of transport before cars. Then they say: もし順番が逆だったら、私は今のように田舎の鉄道の旅を楽しめなかっただろう。この歴史に感謝したい. So the main point is being thankful that trains spread before cars as transportation. That is expressed by option 4.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 「日本では、田舎をゆっくり走る鉄道の旅を多くの人に楽しんでもらいたい。」= In Japan, I want many people to enjoy slow train trips through the countryside.
→ The passage says the writer personally likes such trips, but it does not focus on wanting many other people to enjoy them.

2. 「日本では、かなりの田舎に住んでいても、鉄道が通っているので便利だ。」= In Japan it is convenient because even if you live in quite rural areas, trains run there.
→ This is mentioned (鉄道が通っている) but the writer’s main conclusion is gratitude for the historical order of development, not convenience.

3. 「日本では、自動車の旅も鉄道の旅も楽しめるようになったので、うれしい。」= In Japan I am happy because we can enjoy both car trips and train trips.
→ The text does not say that car trips are enjoyable or focus on enjoying both. The key point is specifically enjoying rural train travel thanks to railways being built earlier.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**
・〜だろう: used here to express speculation about a different past situation (if the order had been reversed...).
・この歴史に感謝したい: I want to be thankful for this history; shows the author’s main feeling.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 — 일본에서는 철도가 자동차보다 먼저 이동 수단으로 널리 쓰이게 되어 다행이다.
근거: 「もし順番が逆だったら、私は今のように田舎の鉄道の旅を楽しめなかっただろう。この歴史に感謝したい」(순서가 반대였다면 시골 철도 여행을 즐기지 못했을 것이다. 이 역사에 감사하고 싶다).
오답: 1 권유, 2 편리함, 3 자동차·철도 양쪽 즐김은 모두 글의 핵심 주장이 아님.

questions.stem: ①「えっ」と思ってとあるが、どうしてそう思ったのか。
opts: パーティーなのに、名刺を渡されてから
名刺だと言われたのに、クッキーを渡されたから
クッキーに名前と会社名が書いてあったから
名刺なのに食べられると言われたから
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**
Answer 2 is correct. It means: Because he was told it was a business card but was handed a cookie.

**Question Translation**
「えっ」と思ってとあるが、どうしてそう思ったのか。
→ It says he thought 'Eh?' (was surprised); why did he think that?

**Explanation**
At the party, the person says this is my business card and then gives him a cookie in a bag. At that moment the writer is surprised: he expected a normal paper business card, but received a cookie instead. That mismatch is exactly what option 2 describes.

Only after that does he look closely and notice the name and company on the cookie, and then is told he can eat it.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**
1. 「パーティーなのに、名刺を渡されてから」= Because even though it was a party, he was given a business card.
